deadlines.notes: Electronic submission via OpenGov portal required. System will not allow uploads after closing time. Virtual RFP opening conducted simultaneously via Google Meet. No proposal may be withdrawn for 60 days after this date. Only 21 days from first publication to due date — tight turnaround.

achievements: Reduced document processing time by 80%, from 8-12 hours to 1.5-2 hours
Improved data extraction accuracy to 95%, virtually eliminating manual rework
Platform processes documents from over 500 institutions simultaneously without latency
Delivered over $800,000 in annual labor cost savings
Achieved 180% ROI within 14 months of launch
User satisfaction scores increased by over 40%
Ensured SLA compliance even during peak academic seasons
project.description: Designed and implemented a serverless enterprise architecture using over 20 AWS services that enabled DegreeData to automate academic document processing, credential verification, and transcript evaluation. The platform transformed document-heavy workflows into a scalable, automated, and intelligent system serving over 2,500 colleges, universities, and professional organizations globally.
project.title: Enterprise Educational Data Platform
project.technicalApproach: Built serverless compute layer with AWS Lambda and AWS Fargate orchestrated through Step Functions for sequential and parallel workflows. Integrated Amazon Textract for structured data extraction from scanned transcripts and forms with over 95% accuracy including handwritten notes. AWS Comprehend provided NLP to classify and extract academic attributes (course titles, grades, GPAs). Amazon Bedrock powered a custom document parser and RAG model to convert unstructured data into JSON for RDS ingestion. Amazon OpenSearch enabled real-time search across millions of academic records. Amazon QuickSight provided dynamic dashboards for processing queues, data quality metrics, and SLA monitoring. Data layer used RDS MySQL for transactional data, DynamoDB for semi-structured records and metadata, S3 for document storage, and ElastiCache for institutional templates. Security via WAF, Shield, Certificate Manager, KMS, Secrets Manager. API Gateway and AppSync for real-time client access. EventBridge and SQS/SNS for async microservice communication. CI/CD with CloudFormation, CodePipeline, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy.

Quality online help documents and job-aids
Reports or other abilities in monitoring system performance including resources, processes, and jobs

Provide updates that are easy to access and install
Provide documentation on all stored procedures and functions including purpose, parameters, and usage samples
Provide on-going technical support for development of new metrics, integration of new data sources, and performance of the system
Data lake capabilities enabling SBCUSD to securely upload, store, and manage ad hoc datasets from various sources supporting structured and unstructured data formats

Enable the District to design and manage intervention programs, provide dashboards evaluating intervention effectiveness, and support creation of individualized student plans for continuous progress monitoring

data.basisOfEstimate: This is a formal RFP (RFP No. 26-22) from San Bernardino City Unified School District for a commercially available, 100% hosted data warehouse and BI/analytics platform. No government estimated value is stated in the solicitation. No organizational labor rates relevant to this type of COTS product delivery are applicable — HORUSTECH's rates ($171.40/hr Software Engineer, $178.89/hr Manufacturing Engineer) are for custom development services, not SaaS product licensing. The pricing structure (Section 8) reveals this is a per-pupil annual license model with hosting fees, implementation fees, and training hours — a commercial software pricing model, not a labor-hour services model. The solicitation requires an existing product with pre-built connectors to Aeries SIS, CAASPP, ELPAC, NWEA MAP, and 16+ other K-12 data sources. HORUSTECH's knowledge base and past performance show no evidence of such a product or K-12 BI platform experience. The DegreeData project ($800K) involved document processing automation for higher education credentials, which is fundamentally different from a district-wide data warehouse/BI platform serving 44,000 students. Price is set to $0 because HORUSTECH cannot credibly price a product it does not have, and building one from scratch would not be competitive against established vendors in this market.
assumptions: HORUSTECH does not currently possess a commercially available K-12 data warehouse and BI/analytics platform product, based on the knowledge base and past performance records reviewed, which show custom development services rather than COTS product offerings.

The solicitation requires an existing, proven product with pre-built integrations to 20+ K-12 education data sources — this is not a custom development engagement where HORUSTECH's software engineering capabilities would be directly applicable.

The mandatory 5-year experience requirement and 5 similar completed projects requirement (Section 6.12, Supplemental Questionnaire Section 6.3) would disqualify HORUSTECH as a prime contractor, as no comparable K-12 BI platform deployments are evidenced in the organization's past performance.

Market pricing for K-12 data warehouse/BI platforms for a district of 44,000 students typically ranges from $150,000-$400,000 annually (including licensing, hosting, support, and training), based on the per-pupil pricing model structure shown in Section 8. Total 5-year contract value would likely be $750,000-$2,000,000 for established vendors.

Cost is weighted at 40% of evaluation (40 points), making it the single most important criterion. Even if HORUSTECH could meet technical requirements, a custom-build price would be non-competitive against established SaaS vendors with amortized development costs.
pricingRisks: INDUSTRY MISMATCH: This RFP requires a mature, commercially available hosted data warehouse and BI/analytics platform (COTS product) with pre-built integrations to over 20 K-12 education data sources (Aeries SIS, CAASPP, ELPAC, NWEA MAP, etc.). HORUSTECH's knowledge base shows custom software development capabilities (AWS serverless, Node.js, TypeScript) but no evidence of an existing K-12 data warehouse/BI product. Building such a platform from scratch would be prohibitively expensive and would not meet the requirement for 5+ years of experience providing similar services to comparable school districts.

EXPERIENCE REQUIREMENT DISQUALIFICATION: Section 6.12 and the Supplemental Questionnaire require the vendor to have been in business providing similar services for at least 5 years and to have completed at least 5 similar projects in both dollar amount and scope. HORUSTECH has one tangentially related EdTech project (DegreeData - document processing, not BI/analytics) and no evidence of K-12 data warehouse deployments, making the firm non-responsive to this mandatory qualification criterion.

COST COMPETITIVENESS: Established K-12 data warehouse/BI vendors (e.g., Schoolzilla/Renaissance, Tableau + Snowflake integrators, Edfi-based solutions, Otus, Illuminate) have amortized development costs across many districts and offer per-pupil SaaS pricing models. A custom-build approach using HORUSTECH's labor rates ($171/hr for software engineers) would result in a price far exceeding market rates for this type of solution, where annual per-pupil costs typically range from $2-$8 for established platforms.

COMPLIANCE AND INSURANCE BURDEN: The RFP requires Cyber Security and Privacy Liability insurance ($1M/$2M), Professional E&O ($1M/$2M with 5-year tail), FERPA compliance, and AB 1584 compliance. These are standard for established EdTech vendors but represent significant overhead for a custom development firm entering this market for the first time.
recommendedActions: NO-BID RECOMMENDATION: This opportunity should be declined. The RFP requires a commercially available, hosted K-12 data warehouse and BI platform with pre-built integrations to 20+ education data sources, 5+ years of experience, and 5+ similar completed projects. HORUSTECH does not have an existing product in this space and cannot meet the mandatory qualification requirements.

MARKET POSITIONING: If HORUSTECH wishes to enter the K-12 EdTech analytics market in the future, consider partnering with an established data warehouse vendor (e.g., Snowflake, Databricks) to build a K-12-specific analytics layer, or pursue subcontracting opportunities with established K-12 BI vendors who need custom integration development work.

ALTERNATIVE OPPORTUNITY PURSUIT: Monitor for RFPs that align with HORUSTECH's actual capabilities — custom software development, data migration, serverless architecture, and document processing automation. The DegreeData past performance is relevant to higher-education document processing, not K-12 district-wide BI platforms.

TEAMING ARRANGEMENT: If HORUSTECH is determined to pursue K-12 education analytics, identify an established K-12 data warehouse vendor and propose as a subcontractor for custom integration or data pipeline development work, leveraging AWS and data engineering expertise without needing to be the prime contractor with the product.

data.overview: San Bernardino City Unified School District (SBCUSD), the ninth-largest public school district in California serving approximately 44,000 students across 74 schools, is soliciting proposals under RFP No. 26-22 for a Data Management and Business Intelligence Platform Solution. The District's Accountability & Educational Technology and Information Technology Departments seek a 100% hosted, integrated data warehouse and business intelligence (BI)/analytics platform to replace or enhance their current PowerSchool Analytics and Insights environment, addressing the need for a more flexible and scalable architecture with data lake functionality to ingest structured and semi-structured data from numerous sources including Aeries SIS, CAASPP, ELPAC, NWEA MAP, College Board Suite, and many others. Success is defined by the District's ability to create and disseminate reports and dashboards that support program evaluation, KPI monitoring, early warning systems, MTSS/RTI, college and career readiness tracking, and data-driven decision making at all organizational levels with timely, accurate, and relevant data. The contract has an initial one-year term with up to four one-year extensions (five years maximum), pricing is sealed and most heavily weighted at 40% of evaluation, proposals must be submitted electronically via the OpenGov e-Procurement portal by March 20, 2026 at 11:00 AM PST, and the vendor must demonstrate at least five years of experience providing similar services to school districts of comparable size and scope. The solution must integrate with over 20 identified student data sources, support Windows, Mac, and mobile platforms, provide multi-level security with role-based access, comply with FERPA and all applicable federal and state laws, and include comprehensive training, implementation planning, disaster recovery, and ongoing technical support.

redFlags.whyItMatters: The Scope of Work (page 14) explicitly identifies PowerSchool Analytics and Insights as the District's current data warehouse and analytics platform, noting it includes 'District specific customizations, completed historical data migration, and automated nightly data refreshes.' This is a significant red flag because any new vendor must replicate or exceed these existing capabilities while also managing a complex data migration. The District's requirement to 'import 5 years of historical data' (Section F.12, page 17) means the winning vendor must extract and transform data from the existing PowerSchool environment. PowerSchool or its authorized partners will have inherent advantages in understanding the existing data structures, customizations, and integration points, and can likely offer a lower-cost, lower-risk continuation proposal. The 40% weight on Cost of Services (Section 5.3, page 7) further amplifies this advantage since the incumbent product vendor avoids migration and re-implementation costs.
redFlags.mitigation: The business development lead should assess whether HORUSTECH can offer a compelling value proposition that addresses the District's explicitly stated desire to 'advance toward a more flexible and scalable architecture that incorporates data lake functionality' and support 'third-party business intelligence platforms' (page 14), which suggests the District may be seeking capabilities beyond what PowerSchool currently provides. If the firm cannot demonstrate clear technical superiority in these areas with competitive pricing, the capture team should seriously consider a no-bid decision. If proceeding, the proposal must include a detailed, risk-mitigated transition plan with specific milestones and a data migration strategy.
redFlags.flag: Current PowerSchool Analytics platform creates a strong incumbent product advantage with established customizations and data pipelines.

redFlags.whyItMatters: Section 6.12 (page 9) states: 'The Respondent, by submitting a RFP, indicates that they have the ability, fitness and expertise to fulfill the terms and conditions of the RFP, and has been successful in services associated with this RFP for a period of at least (5) years.' Additionally, the Supplemental Questionnaire (Section 6.3 in Document 8) requires 'proof that the bidder has been in business under the same license and company name for at least five consecutive years and shall have successfully completed at least five (5) similar, both dollar amount and scope of work, projects.' This is a potential disqualifier if HORUSTECH has not been operating under its current name for five years or cannot demonstrate five completed K-12 data warehouse/BI projects of similar dollar amount and scope. Failure to meet this threshold is explicitly stated as 'cause for rejection of the bid' in Section 6.3 of the OpenGov portal instructions.
redFlags.mitigation: The capture manager must immediately verify HORUSTECH's incorporation date and confirm whether the firm has operated under its current name and license for at least five consecutive years. If the firm does not meet this threshold, the team should evaluate whether a teaming arrangement with a prime contractor who meets the requirement is feasible, or whether this constitutes a no-bid condition. Legal counsel should review whether the five-year requirement is a hard gate or a representation that could be addressed through team qualifications, and a clarification question should be submitted via OpenGov before the March 13 deadline.
redFlags.flag: Five-year minimum experience requirement in similar services may be a disqualifying barrier.

redFlags.whyItMatters: The OpenGov portal instructions (Document 8, Vendor Submission Item 3) explicitly warn: 'NOTE: There will be a 10 point penalty if pricing is included in this section.' The proposal must be uploaded without any fee proposal or pricing information in the main proposal document, with cost breakdowns submitted separately in Vendor Submission Item 4. Given that the total evaluation is out of 100 points and the margin between competitive proposals can be narrow, a 10-point penalty would be devastating — effectively eliminating the firm from contention. This is a compliance trap because vendors accustomed to including pricing context within technical narratives may inadvertently reference specific dollar amounts, per-student costs, or fee structures in their main proposal document. The sealed bid process (confirmed in Document 7 and 8) means pricing is not disclosed until after technical evaluation, making this separation critical.
redFlags.mitigation: The proposal manager must implement a strict quality control review specifically checking for any pricing references, dollar amounts, cost estimates, or fee language in the main proposal document before upload. A dedicated reviewer who did not author the proposal should perform this check at least 48 hours before the March 20 deadline. The team should create separate document templates for the technical proposal and fee proposal to prevent accidental cross-contamination of pricing information.
redFlags.flag: 10-point penalty for including pricing in the wrong proposal section creates a compliance trap.

risks.whyItMatters: Per the Notice Inviting Proposals (Document 5, page 2), the RFP was posted on February 27, 2026 with a due date of March 20, 2026 at 11:00 AM PST, providing only 21 calendar days for response. The question deadline is March 13, 2026 (Section 2.2, page 4), leaving only 14 days to review the extensive scope of work, formulate clarification questions, and receive answers before the final submission. Given the complexity of the requirements across six functional categories (Solution Requirements, Security, Data Quality, BI Reporting, Implementation/Training, and Data Integration per pages 15-18), this compressed timeline makes it challenging to prepare a thorough, competitive proposal that addresses all required and desired features while also completing the 13+ mandatory certification attachments listed in Section 4.0(J).
risks.mitigation: The capture manager should immediately assign dedicated team members to begin drafting responses to each of the six scope categories (Sections A through F on pages 15-18) and the Qualification Statement (page 20) in parallel. The proposal coordinator should submit all clarification questions via the OpenGov Question/Answer tab no later than March 10, 2026, three days before the March 13 deadline, to allow time for follow-up. Administrative staff should begin completing the 13 mandatory certification forms (Attachments A through M) immediately to avoid last-minute bottlenecks.
risks.flag: Short response window of 21 calendar days limits proposal preparation time.

risks.whyItMatters: The Scope of Work (Document 5, page 14) explicitly states that 'The District's current environment utilizes PowerSchool Analytics and Insights (formerly Unified Insights) as its data warehouse and analytics platform. The implementation includes District specific customizations, completed historical data migration, and automated nightly data refreshes.' This means the incumbent product vendor has deep institutional knowledge of SBCUSD's data environment, existing integrations with AERIES and other data sources listed in Table 1 (page 18), and established relationships with District staff. Any competing vendor must account for data migration from the existing PowerSchool environment, re-implementation of District-specific customizations, and re-establishment of automated data pipelines — all of which add cost and risk that the incumbent does not face. The evaluation criteria in Section 5.3 (page 7) weights 'Cost of Services' at 40%, and the incumbent's lower transition costs could provide a significant pricing advantage.
risks.mitigation: The business development lead should immediately research PowerSchool Analytics and Insights capabilities and pricing to understand the competitive landscape and identify differentiation opportunities, particularly around the District's stated desire for 'data lake functionality' and 'third-party business intelligence platforms' (page 14) which suggest dissatisfaction with current capabilities. The proposal team should emphasize any superior data lake, advanced analytics, and third-party BI interoperability features in the response to Scope of Work Section D items 18 and 20 (page 16), and provide a detailed, low-risk transition plan per Qualification Statement Section A.8 (page 21) to mitigate the District's concerns about switching costs.
risks.flag: Existing PowerSchool Analytics and Insights platform creates significant incumbent product advantage.

risks.whyItMatters: Section 5.3 (page 7) allocates 15 points (15% of total score) to 'Firm's expertise, background, personnel qualifications, and experience with School Districts similar in size and scope to SBCUSD.' Section 4.0(E) (pages 5-6) requires 'a list of other school districts or county offices of education for which services have been performed in the last five (5) years' and asks proposers to 'identify districts that are of the approximate size of the District' (approximately 44,000 students). Section 6.12 (page 9) further requires that the respondent 'has been successful in services associated with this RFP for a period of at least (5) years.' Based on available past performance records, HORUSTECH's closest relevant project is an EdTech data platform for DegreeData, which is not a K-12 school district. Without direct K-12 district references of comparable size, the firm risks scoring poorly on 15% of the evaluation and may not meet the five-year experience threshold, potentially resulting in proposal rejection.
risks.mitigation: The capture lead should conduct an immediate internal audit to identify any K-12 school district engagements, subcontractor relationships, or team member experience that could be cited. If direct K-12 experience is insufficient, the proposal team should consider partnering with or subcontracting to a firm that has established K-12 data warehouse implementations with districts of 30,000+ students, and this teaming arrangement should be finalized before the March 13 question deadline. The proposal should also highlight the DegreeData project's educational domain relevance and emphasize transferable capabilities in data integration, analytics, and FERPA-compliant data handling.
risks.flag: Limited K-12 school district past performance may not satisfy experience requirements.

risks.whyItMatters: Section F (Data Integration/User Interface, pages 17-18) and Table 1 (page 18) require integration with approximately 20+ distinct data sources including AERIES Student Information System (marked as a critical data source), CAASPP, ELPAC, Illuminate, SEIS, itsLearning LMS, NWEA MAP, Renaissance STAR, College Board Suite, DIBELS, IXL, Google Suite, and others. Several of these are marked as 'Critical Data Sources' (denoted with 'c' in Table 1). Requirements F.1, F.5, F.6, and F.7 (page 17) mandate real-time and nightly data refreshing with AERIES and the ability to provide integration scripts. Requirement F.12 requires importing 5 years of historical data. These integrations are highly specific to the California K-12 education technology ecosystem, and failure to demonstrate existing connectors or integration experience with these systems would significantly weaken the technical proposal scored under the 25-point 'Responses to Scope of Work' criterion (Section 5.3, page 7).
risks.mitigation: The technical lead should immediately inventory which of the 20+ data sources in Table 1 the firm has existing integration experience with, and identify gaps that require development or partnership. For critical data sources (AERIES, Illuminate, DIBELS), the firm should explore API documentation and potential technology partnerships with vendors who have pre-built connectors. The proposal should include a detailed integration architecture diagram and timeline addressing each data source, with particular emphasis on the critical sources, to demonstrate technical feasibility to evaluators by the March 20 submission deadline.
risks.flag: Extensive data source integration requirements demand proven K-12 ecosystem expertise.

risks.whyItMatters: Section 7.0 (page 11) requires four distinct insurance policies: Commercial General Liability at $1,000,000/$2,000,000 (Section 7.1), Commercial Auto Liability at $1,000,000 (Section 7.2), Professional Errors and Omissions at $1,000,000/$2,000,000 with a 5-year discovery tail (Section 7.3), and Cyber Security and Privacy Liability at $1,000,000/$2,000,000 (Section 7.4), plus Workers' Compensation per California Labor Code (Section 7.5). All insurers must be California Admitted with an A.M. Best rating of A or better. The Cyber Security and Privacy Liability requirement is particularly notable as it must cover PII, PHI, notification costs, crisis management, regulatory fines, and network security liability. If the firm does not currently carry all of these policies at the required limits, obtaining or increasing coverage can take several weeks and may involve significant premium costs that must be factored into pricing.
risks.mitigation: The operations or finance lead should immediately review current insurance certificates against the five specific requirements in Section 7.0 and identify any gaps, particularly for the Cyber Security and Privacy Liability policy with its specific coverage requirements. The firm's insurance broker should be contacted within the first week to obtain quotes and confirm that all policies can be bound with California Admitted carriers rated A or better by A.M. Best before contract execution. Premium costs should be communicated to the pricing team for inclusion in the fee proposal.
risks.flag: Comprehensive insurance requirements including cyber liability may require lead time to secure.

risks.whyItMatters: Section 5.6 (page 8) states the District 'may, at its sole discretion, shortlist selected bidders for virtual presentations' anticipated in April 2026 per the Schedule of Events (Section 5.4, page 7). The section further requires that 'Firms invited for oral presentations must be represented by the individual who will be the prime contact person to the District, and other key project team members,' and warns that 'A Proposer's inability to respond to any request for clarification and/or oral presentation may be cause for disqualification.' While the presentation is not guaranteed, if shortlisted, the firm must ensure that the named Project Leader, Implementation Team lead, and Customer Service Key Contact (as identified in Qualification Statement Section A.2, page 20) are available throughout April 2026. Failure to present with the named personnel could result in disqualification.
risks.mitigation: The proposal manager should confirm availability of all key personnel named in the proposal for the entire month of April 2026 before finalizing the Personnel and Staffing Resources section (Section 4.0(C), page 5). The team should prepare a presentation framework and demo environment in parallel with proposal development so that if shortlisted, the firm can respond quickly to a presentation scheduling request from the District.
risks.flag: Potential oral presentation requirement with key personnel availability constraints.

incumbentInfo.notes: The Scope of Work (page 14) identifies PowerSchool Analytics and Insights (formerly Unified Insights) as the District's current data warehouse and analytics platform product. However, the solicitation does not name the company or contractor that holds the current contract for this service — PowerSchool is the product/platform, not necessarily the contracting entity. The solicitation does not use terms like 'recompete', 'follow-on', or 'predecessor contract'. The District appears to be conducting an open competitive procurement to potentially replace or upgrade its current platform.

criteria.name: TECHNICAL_FIT
criteria.rationale: This RFP requires a commercially available, 100% hosted K-12 data warehouse and BI/analytics platform with pre-built integrations to 20+ education-specific data sources (Aeries SIS, CAASPP, ELPAC, NWEA MAP, etc.). HORUSTECH's KB shows custom software development capabilities (AWS serverless, Node.js, TypeScript) but no evidence of an existing K-12 data warehouse/BI product. The company builds custom solutions, not COTS platforms. The gap analysis shows only 10% coverage of requirements with 76 critical gaps identified. Key requirements like natural language querying, GIS mapping, pre-built K-12 reports, nightly Aeries roster refreshing, and language translation of dashboards have no matching capability in the KB.

criteria.name: PAST_PERFORMANCE_RELEVANCE
criteria.rationale: The only matched project is the DegreeData 'Enterprise Educational Data Platform' with a 53% relevance score, but this was a document processing automation system for higher education credential verification — fundamentally different from a K-12 district-wide data warehouse/BI platform serving 44,000 students. The domain similarity is only 20% (higher ed document processing vs. K-12 analytics). HORUSTECH has zero K-12 school district references, zero data warehouse/BI platform deployments, and cannot meet the mandatory requirement of 5 similar completed projects in both dollar amount and scope. The overall past performance coverage is only 10%, with 76 critical gaps. The RFP allocates 15% of evaluation specifically to experience with similarly-sized school districts.
gaps: No K-12 school district past performance
Cannot provide 3 required school district references
Cannot demonstrate 5 years of similar services
Cannot demonstrate 5 similar completed projects
No data warehouse/BI platform deployment experience

criteria.name: PRICING_POSITION
criteria.rationale: The pricing analysis conclusively demonstrates HORUSTECH cannot competitively price this opportunity. The RFP requires a per-pupil SaaS pricing model for an existing product, not custom development labor rates. HORUSTECH's labor rates ($171/hr) applied to building a platform from scratch would far exceed market rates of $2-$8 per pupil annually from established vendors. The pricing module set total price to $0 with only 18% confidence because HORUSTECH does not possess a product to price. Cost is weighted at 40% of evaluation — the single most important criterion — making this a fatal competitive disadvantage. Established K-12 BI vendors have amortized development costs across hundreds of districts.
gaps: No existing product to price competitively
Custom development approach non-competitive against SaaS vendors
Cannot match per-pupil pricing model of established competitors
Implementation/migration costs would be prohibitive

criteria.name: STRATEGIC_ALIGNMENT
criteria.rationale: No strategic plan or market expansion goals are documented in the KB or organization context that indicate HORUSTECH intends to enter the K-12 education data warehouse/BI market. The company's documented capabilities are in custom software development, serverless architecture, and document processing — not COTS product delivery for school districts. Pursuing this opportunity would require building an entirely new product line from scratch, which is not evidenced as a strategic objective. The pricing analysis explicitly recommends NO-BID and suggests monitoring for opportunities aligned with actual capabilities. There is no documented strategic rationale to justify the investment required to compete.
gaps: No documented strategy to enter K-12 BI market
No product roadmap for data warehouse/BI platform
Opportunity requires COTS product, not custom development services
No evidence of K-12 market expansion goals

criteria.name: INCUMBENT_RISK
criteria.rationale: PowerSchool Analytics and Insights is the current platform with established District-specific customizations, completed historical data migration, and automated nightly data refreshes already in place. While PowerSchool is the product (not necessarily the contracting entity), any vendor offering PowerSchool or its successor has massive advantages: no migration risk, no re-implementation costs, existing data pipelines, and institutional knowledge. The 40% cost weighting heavily favors the incumbent since they avoid transition costs. Additionally, established K-12 BI vendors (Schoolzilla/Renaissance, Otus, Illuminate) have pre-built connectors and district references that HORUSTECH completely lacks. The competitive landscape is extremely unfavorable.
gaps: PowerSchool incumbent product advantage with existing customizations
Established K-12 BI vendors have pre-built connectors and references
Incumbent avoids migration costs giving 40%-weighted pricing advantage

data.summary: San Bernardino City Unified School District (SBCUSD), the ninth-largest public school district in California serving approximately 44,000 students across 74 schools, is seeking a fully hosted integrated data warehouse and business intelligence (BI)/analytics platform to replace its current PowerSchool Analytics and Insights system. The solution must integrate data from over 20 student data sources—including Aeries SIS, CAASPP, ELPAC, NWEA MAP, College Board Suite, National Student Clearinghouse, Renaissance STAR, SEIS, and Google Suite—with nightly roster refreshes, real-time demographic updates, and import of 5 years of historical data, while supporting data lake capabilities for structured and semi-structured data ingestion. Key technical requirements include multi-level role-based security, Active Directory authentication, catastrophic event recovery within one week, natural language querying, GIS mapping, predictive analytics, language translation of dashboards, and compatibility with Windows, Mac, mobile platforms, and HP server hardware. The contract is structured as an initial one-year term with four successive one-year extension options at the District's sole discretion, for a maximum cumulative period of five years, with pricing held firm except for District-approved inflationary adjustments. Proposals are evaluated on a 100-point scale weighted heavily toward cost of services (40%), scope of work responses (25%), firm expertise with similarly sized school districts (15%), and training/support (10%). This procurement supports SBCUSD's strategic initiatives including program evaluation, early warning systems, MTSS/RTI, college and career readiness monitoring, District KPI tracking, and intervention effectiveness dashboards to drive data-informed decision-making at all organizational levels. The vendor must comply with FERPA, California Education Code AB 1584, and all applicable federal, state, and local regulations, and must provide comprehensive training, technical support, and an implementation plan with milestones.
data.periodOfPerformance: 1 year initial term + 4 one-year extension options (up to 5 years total)
